meta.method: rubric + AI-assisted (Claude), grounded in deterministic metrics
meta.pilotScope: Ying Wu College of Computing (degree programs)

unevaluated.note: Other colleges are a follow-up run of the same pipeline.

b-a-in-computer-science-226.name: B.A. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan follows a well-ordered CS spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288/CS 331/CS 332 -> CS 350/CS 356 -> CS 435/CS 490 -> CS 491 Senior Project) with a matching math track (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 333), and prerequisite depths climb smoothly from 0 to 6 with no abrupt jumps. As a B.A. it leans on generic elective placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') that leave the upper-level path less prescribed than the core.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the repeated generic elective placeholders into named recommended tracks or example course lists so students see coherent upper-level pathways rather than open slots.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensibly shaped with a deepest chain of 6 (CS 100->113->114->241->288/... -> CS 435 -> CS 490 -> CS 491), which is reasonable for a 4-year CS degree and shows no over- or under-constraining in the core. The 5 dangling references (missingCourseCount 5: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, a lab, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine CS prerequisite breaks, so structural health is good but not clean.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or explicit code mappings for YWCC 207, YWCC 307 and PHYS 111A, and resolve the GER 200/GER 300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a term-by-term plan of study and per-course descriptions are present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes / educational objectives (e.g., what a graduate can design, analyze, and communicate) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded (14-16 credits each). The mix transparently spans a CS core, a math/science block, GER requirements, and multiple free electives appropriate to a B.A.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. GER vs. free electives) so the balance implied by the term plan is stated explicitly for students.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and required courses are legible, but the plan contains parsing artifacts that hurt clarity: several GER items are split across rows with catalog URLs bleeding into titles (e.g., 'Science with Lab Elective (http://catalog...' followed by 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)') and a stray 'B.A.'-context line '228 B.S. in Computer Science' appears mid-plan, plus a null-code FYS seminar row.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean up the plan-of-study rendering so GER titles are single legible entries without embedded URLs and remove the page-number/cross-program artifact ('228 B.S. in Computer Science').

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Required courses cover current, employable fundamentals: databases (CS 331), operating systems (CS 332), networks (CS 356), computer systems (CS 350), Linux/Python/C/MySQL tooling (CS 288), advanced algorithms (CS 435), software engineering (CS 490), and a team Senior Project (CS 491). Notable modern gaps in the required core are AI/ML, cybersecurity, and cloud/data-science, which the B.A. leaves to unspecified electives, and CS 288 still foregrounds PHP.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface or require modern electives in AI/ML, security, and cloud/data engineering (or define a track), and refresh CS 288's stack emphasis away from dated PHP framing.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course has a present, specific description with real technical substance (e.g., CS 241 on algorithm design/proof and complexity, CS 356 on layered architectures/routing, CS 435 on graphs/hash tables), and none are thin or placeholder. A few (MATH 111/112) are terse topic lists rather than full descriptions.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with sensible, explicit grade conditions and OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better'), and the core chain is internally consistent. Clarity is dinged by inconsistent phrasing ('grade C or better' vs 'grade of C or higher'), the verbose IS 350 GER clause, and prerequisites that point at dangling GER placeholders.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives on the catalog page, which are currently entirely absent.
Clean up plan-of-study parsing artifacts: GER titles broken across rows with embedded catalog URLs and the stray 'B.S. in Computer Science' page-number line that bleeds into this B.A. plan.
Resolve the 5 dangling course references (YWCC 207, YWCC 307, PHYS 111A, GER 200, GER 300) by adding or mapping proper catalog entries.
Strengthen currency of the required/elective set by surfacing or requiring modern areas (AI/ML, cybersecurity, cloud/data science) and refreshing CS 288's dated PHP emphasis.

Replace generic elective placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') with named recommended courses or defined tracks and standardize prerequisite grade-condition wording.

b-a-in-information-systems-249.name: B.A. in Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan of study lays out a clear, well-ordered IS spine across eight labeled terms: foundational computing and web work (CS 100, IS 117, IS 218, IS 265, IS 247) progress into database, requirements, and UX-research courses (IS 331, IS 390, IS 375, IS 344), and culminate in management and advanced/capstone work (IS 455, IS 465, IS 491). Prerequisite depth (deepest chain of 7, with IS 465 terminal) confirms courses genuinely build on one another term over term with no abrupt jumps. The main weakness is that later years lean heavily on unlabeled placeholder buckets (Career Track, New Media/Business Specialization, and General Electives), which leaves part of the upper-level progression unspecified.
dimensions.suggestion: Attach a concrete example course list to each elective bucket (Career Track, Specialization, General) so the upper-division progression is as legible as the required IS core.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and sensible: a deepest chain of 7 reflects a genuine multi-year build-up (e.g., IS 465 requires a statistical GER plus IS 265, IS 344, and IS 331/CS 331) without appearing over-constrained. Prerequisites are richly specified with explicit grade conditions and boolean logic. Only 3 of 28 referenced codes are missing from the catalog (missingCourseCount=3: GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), and all three are peripheral — a general-education category reference and two 1-credit professional-development seminars — rather than breaks in the core IS chain.
dimensions.suggestion: Reconcile the three dangling codes (GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) with the undergraduate catalog by either linking them to real course entries or replacing the GER placeholder with the specific requirement it points to.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present in the data (learningOutcomes is null). For a catalog page, the absence of stated outcomes is a real finding: prospective students, advisors, and accreditors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do, and the plan of study alone does not substitute for explicit competency statements.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes (e.g., 5-8 measurable statements covering systems analysis, database design, UX, and IS management) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summed term credits (120) equal the stated total (120), so creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 — internally consistent with no missing or double-counted credits. Term loads are reasonable and steady (mostly 15-16 credits, with a lighter 12-credit final term), and the mix spans general education (English, math, natural science, history/humanities GERs), a substantial IS major core, and multiple elective categories. The score is held just below exemplary because the numerous unlabeled elective buckets make the precise gen-ed / major-core / elective split hard to verify even though the totals reconcile.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. general education vs. free/track electives) so the balance behind the reconciled 120 total is transparent at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan of study is present with clearly labeled terms and per-item credits, which aids readability. However, completeness and structure suffer: there is no program-level description or overview narrative at all, and several rows are parsing artifacts — GER references have their URLs split across multiple line items with null course codes and null credits (e.g., a 'Natural Science GER (http://...' row followed by a stray 'requirements/natural-science-ger/)' row). These broken fragments and the missing overview undercut an otherwise legible plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a concise program overview paragraph and clean up the fragmented GER rows so each requirement renders as a single, complete line rather than split URL fragments.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current IS practice well: two dedicated UX courses (IS 247 Designing the User Experience, IS 375 Discovering User Needs for UX), hands-on web application development (IS 117, IS 218), database design (IS 331), requirements analysis and systems design (IS 390), IS management and business processes (IS 455), and an advanced course (IS 465) that explicitly introduces data analysis and data-science techniques. Career-track and New Media/Business specialization electives add contemporary flexibility. Modern gaps are that cybersecurity and cloud computing appear only implicitly (networking is limited to the introductory IT 120) and data analytics is only introductory rather than a named required strand.
dimensions.suggestion: Name current-practice courses in data analytics, cloud, and cybersecurity as explicit required or career-track options rather than leaving them to unlabeled elective buckets.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 25 sampled courses carry substantive, specific description excerpts that convey real topics and scope (e.g., IS 218's Web-based information systems, IS 331's database back-end functionality, IS 465's data-analysis and statistics from an IS perspective) rather than thin placeholders. The only blemish is a short, artifact-laden MATH 138 excerpt ending in stray text ('298 Jordan'); otherwise descriptions are informative and consistent.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unusually clearly and consistently: they include explicit grade conditions ('ENGL 102 with a grade of C or higher') and well-formed boolean logic ('(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'). Clarity is lightly reduced by a few category-level references (e.g., 'Statistical GER', 'History and Humanities GER 200 level') and the 3 dangling codes (GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), which are resolvable but not spelled out as concrete courses.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes (5-8 measurable competency statements); none are currently present.
Add a program overview/description narrative to the catalog page, which currently has none.
Clean up presentation artifacts and reconcile the 3 dangling course codes (GER 200, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), including the GER rows whose URLs are split across null-code line items.
Name current-practice courses in data analytics, cloud, and cybersecurity as explicit required or career-track options rather than leaving them implicit.
Label the elective buckets (General, Career Track, and New Media/Business Specialization) with concrete example course lists so the core-vs-elective split is transparent.

b-s-in-business-and-information-systems-.name: B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan builds logically from foundations (CS 100, IS 117, IS 265) through core IS competencies (IS 218 web apps, IS 331 databases, IS 390 requirements analysis) to advanced/management courses (IS 455 IS Management & Business Processes, IS 465 Advanced Information Systems) and a senior capstone (IS 491/IT 491). Business fundamentals (ACCT, ECON, FIN, MGMT, MRKT, HRM) interleave sensibly with the IS track, and a deepest prerequisite chain of 7 confirms genuine vertical progression. The blend is well-ordered with no abrupt jumps; the only softness is a few unlabeled general-elective and GER slots.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate the general-elective and GER placeholder slots with recommended course options so students see how those choices reinforce the business-plus-IS progression rather than appearing as generic filler.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is real and well-formed, with per-course depths spanning 0 to 7 and a deepestPrereqChain of 7 that reflects a coherent path into advanced IS (IS 465 at depth 7). However, metrics.missingCourseCount is 4, with missingCoursesSample listing GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, and YWCC 307 as dangling references not resolvable in the undergraduate catalog. GER 200/GER 300 are general-education placeholders rather than true course codes, while YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are one-credit seminars that appear in the plan but do not resolve; none break the core IS chain, but they are genuine dangling references, and a chain depth of 7 is on the deep side and worth auditing for over-constraint.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references by mapping GER 200 and GER 300 to their canonical general-education requirement pages and confirming YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are cataloged, then verify the depth-7 chain into IS 465 does not over-constrain scheduling.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no learning-outcomes, objectives, or overview field of any kind; only the structured plan of study and course list are present. For a catalog-quality page, the absence of explicit program-level learning outcomes is a real deficiency because prospective students and assessors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program-level learning outcomes (for example, four to six statements covering IS analysis and design, database and web application development, business process management, and data-driven decision making) directly on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: metrics.creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 and summedTermCredits (120.0) equals statedTotalCredits (120.0), so there is no internal inconsistency. Term loads are well balanced, ranging from 12 to 16 credits with most terms at 15-16, and the mix spans general education (ENGL, MATH, science GER, HSS), a business core (ACCT/ECON/FIN/MGMT/MRKT/HRM), an IS core, and general electives. The one point held back reflects that the gen-ed versus major versus elective totals are not summarized explicitly for the reader.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-distribution summary (e.g., general education X, business core Y, IS core Z, electives W) so the transparent 120-credit total is visible at a glance rather than only inferable from the term-by-term plan.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan is organized into clearly labeled terms (First Year 1st Semester through Fourth Year 2nd Semester) with per-term credit totals, which aids readability. However, several parsing artifacts degrade the page: GER entries are split across multiple rows with raw catalog URL fragments (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER (http://...' followed by 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)'), alternative options render as bare 'or ACCT 115 or...' lines, and many rows carry null codes. There is also no program description or narrative overview.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parsing so GER requirements render as single labeled rows without embedded URLs, collapse the 'or' alternatives into clear either/or pairs, and add a brief program overview paragraph at the top of the page.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current information-systems and business practice: IT 310 E-Commerce Technology, MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics, IS 247 and IS 375 user-experience and user-needs courses, IS 218 Building Web Applications, IS 331 database design, IS 390 requirements analysis, IS 455 IS management and business processes, and IT 120 networking, all grounded in a solid business core (marketing, corporate finance, organizational behavior, international business). This is a modern, employable blend. Notable gaps are the absence of dedicated cybersecurity, cloud, or explicit data-science/AI coursework beyond introductory analytics.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or list an elective option in cybersecurity, cloud computing, or applied machine learning/data science to close the most visible modern gap and keep the analytics thread current.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every one of the 33 sampled courses (ACCT 115 through MRKT 330) has a null description, so no course on the page provides any explanatory text about content, topics, or scope. This is a uniform and serious presentation gap: titles alone must carry all meaning, and several are cryptic abbreviations (e.g., 'Principles Of Fin Accountng', 'IS Mgmt & Business Processes').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite relationships exist and were parsed into a depth graph (course depths range 0 to 7), but no course exposes explicit, human-readable prerequisite text, and metrics.missingCourseCount is 4 with GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, and YWCC 307 dangling. The combination of unstated prerequisite text and dangling references means students cannot reliably see or verify what is required before each course.

topSuggestions: Publish course descriptions for all courses; every one of the 33 sampled courses currently has no description, which is the single largest gap on the page.
Add explicit program-level learning outcomes/objectives, which are entirely absent from the program data.
Resolve the four dangling course references (GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) so prerequisite and requirement links all point to cataloged entries.
Fix plan-of-study parsing artifacts so GER requirements render as single clean rows without embedded URLs and 'or' alternatives display as clear either/or choices.
Add a program overview and a credit-distribution summary (gen-ed vs. business core vs. IS core vs. electives) to frame the transparent, reconciled 120-credit plan.

b-s-in-computer-science-228.name: B.S. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan of study progresses cleanly across 8 terms from CS 100 (Roadmap) to CS 113/114 introductory sequence, into theory (CS 241, CS 341), systems (CS 332, CS 350, CS 356), and a CS 490 -> CS 491 capstone, with math prerequisites (MATH 111/112/333/337) sequenced to support them; deepestPrereqChain of 6 reflects genuine layered build-up rather than a flat list.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish named elective tracks (e.g., AI/data, security, systems) so the four '300+ CS Elective' slots map to coherent specialization pathways instead of undifferentiated choices.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains are well-formed and consistently gated (e.g., CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/280, each 'with a grade C or better'), and a deepestPrereqChain of 6 is sensible for a 120-credit BS; however missingCourseCount is 6, with dangling references including PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, and YWCC 307 (courses that appear in the plan but resolve to no catalog entry) plus GER 200/GER 300 placeholder references.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or link catalog entries for the lab and 1-credit YWCC courses (PHYS 111A/121A, YWCC 207/307) and standardize the GER 200/300 placeholder labels so no referenced code resolves to nothing.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a term-by-term course list and per-course descriptions are present, so a reader cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes / educational objectives on the catalog page (e.g., ABET-style student outcomes) so competencies are stated rather than inferred from course titles.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 and summedTermCredits (120) exactly matches statedTotalCredits (120), with per-term loads tightly balanced between 14 and 16 credits and a visible mix of major core, math/science, GER, and free electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface a one-line credit-category summary (major core vs. GER vs. electives) on the page so the already-balanced distribution is transparent to prospective students at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and per-course descriptions are informative, but the parsed page contains several broken rows: stray URL fragments as course titles ('requirements/natural-science-ger/)', 'general-education-requirements/ger-200-level/)') and orphaned page-break text ('230 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics'), plus null-code/null-credit line noise that fragments the plan of study.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parsing so GER/elective placeholders render as single labeled rows and remove leaked URL fragments and page-break headers from the item list.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Required and elective courses reflect current practice: CS 301 Introduction to Data Science, CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity, CS 356 Computer Networks, CS 331 Database System Design, CS 288 Intensive Programming in Linux (Php/Python/C, Apache/MySQL), and CS 490 software engineering plus a team-based CS 491 senior project, alongside professional-development YWCC 207/307.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit required or clearly-flagged machine learning / AI course to complement the data-science introduction and keep pace with current hiring demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course carries a present, specific, and substantive description (e.g., CS 241 on algorithm correctness and complexity, CS 350 on Von Neumann architecture and numeric representation, CS 356 on layered network architecture and routing); none are thin or placeholder, with only expected truncation of excerpts.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with consistent grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and clear boolean logic (e.g., CS 341 '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280'), but clarity is dented by GER placeholder references in prose (IS 350's 'GER (CS 100, CS 101...)' and 'History and Humanities GER 200 level') and the dangling GER 200/GER 300 codes flagged in metrics.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives on the catalog page, which are currently entirely absent.

Fix the plan-of-study parsing to remove leaked URL fragments and page-break text (e.g., 'requirements/natural-science-ger/)', '230 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics') and render each GER/elective placeholder as one clean labeled row.
Resolve the 6 dangling course references by adding or linking catalog entries for PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, and YWCC 307 and standardizing the GER 200/GER 300 placeholder labels.
Define named CS elective tracks so the four '300+ CS Elective' slots guide students into coherent specializations.
Add an explicitly required AI/machine-learning course to complement CS 301 Data Science and reflect current industry demand.

b-s-in-computer-science-and-b-s-in-appli.name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The five-year plan builds logically along three well-ordered spines: CS (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 341/CS 350/CS 435 -> CS 490/CS 491), physics (PHYS 111 -> 121 -> 234 -> 335 -> 430/432 -> 442/485), and math (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 211 -> 222/335 -> 328), with a deepest prereq chain of 7 confirming genuine term-over-term progression and no abrupt jumps. Integration points such as CS 301 Data Science, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation sensibly tie the two disciplines together.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short narrative or curriculum map showing how the CS and Applied Physics tracks reinforce each other (e.g., which physics courses feed CS 301/PHYS 485) so the dual-degree rationale is explicit rather than implied by co-scheduling.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible with a deepest chain of 7 (reasonable for a rigorous 5-year dual degree) and clean grade conditions throughout. The 7 missing referenced courses (CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A, GER 200, GER 300) are all lab co-requisites or general-education category placeholders rather than genuinely broken CS/physics prerequisite chains, so structural health is strong.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the lab co-requisites (CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) and GER 200/300 category codes in the catalog so these dangling references resolve and missingCourseCount drops toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a plan of study and course list are present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, since students and accreditors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit list of program learning outcomes (e.g., 5-8 statements covering computing foundations, applied-physics modeling, and integrated problem-solving) directly on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta = 0.0; summed term credits 134 = stated 134), and the distribution spans a heavy STEM core (CS, physics, math, chemistry) with writing (ENGL 101/102, COM 312), ethics (IS 350), and GER/HSS requirements woven in. For a combined/dual BS/BS a larger load is expected, and 134 credits sits at the lighter end, which is worth confirming.
dimensions.suggestion: Verify the total against a true dual-degree load and confirm no term is missing (the plan ends at Fifth Year 1st Semester), then annotate the gen-ed vs. major-core vs. elective credit split so the balance is transparent to students.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and per-term credits are shown, but the page carries several PDF-extraction artifacts that hurt readability: GER hyperlinks are split across multiple null-code rows (e.g., 'History and Humanities GER 200 level (http://...' followed by a bare path fragment), 'or MATH 328 / or COM 313' alternatives appear as separate credit-less items, and a stray '232 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences' row bleeds in from an adjacent program.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parse so split hyperlink rows are merged, 'or ...' alternatives are folded into their parent course, and the stray '232 ...' fragment is removed.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Required and elective courses reflect current practice: CS 301 Introduction to Data Science, CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity, CS 356 Computer Networks, CS 331 Databases, CS 435 Advanced Algorithms, CS 490 Software Engineering, and CS 288 (Linux/Python/PHP/MySQL) cover in-demand skills, while PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics and PHYS 485 Computer Modeling keep the physics side modern. A dedicated machine-learning/AI course is the main current gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or cross-list an explicit machine learning / AI elective to complement CS 301, since ML fluency is now a baseline industry expectation for both computing and applied-physics graduates.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled course descriptions are present, specific, and informative, naming concrete topics (e.g., CS 341 covers automata, computability, and complexity; CS 435 covers balanced search trees, hashing, and graph algorithms; MATH 335 covers Gauss/Green/Stokes theorems). None read as thin placeholders, though several excerpts are truncated mid-sentence.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with consistent grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and sensible alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114'), and introductory courses correctly show no prereqs. Clarity is only modestly reduced by references to lab co-reqs and GER categories that are not themselves catalogued.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes/educational objectives on the catalog page (currently none are present).

Clean up the plan-of-study parsing artifacts: merge split GER hyperlink rows, fold 'or ...' course alternatives into their parent entries, and remove the stray '232 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences' fragment.
Resolve the 7 dangling references by registering lab co-requisites (CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) and GER 200/300 placeholders in the catalog.
Verify the 134-credit total for a dual BS/BS and confirm no final term is missing (the plan currently ends at Fifth Year 1st Semester), annotating the gen-ed/major-core/elective split.
Add an explicit machine learning / AI elective to modernize the computing track alongside CS 301 Data Science.

b-s-in-computer-science-and-b-s-in-mathe.name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences, Applied Mathematics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Within the visible terms the sequence is sound: the CS spine builds logically (CS 114 to CS 280 to CS 288 to CS 350 to CS 435/CS 341) and the math spine progresses through Calculus III, Differential Equations, and Vector Analysis before capstone work (CS 491 Senior Project). As a 5-year combined degree the heavier load and longer horizon are expected. Two issues hold it to adequate: the parsed plan begins at 'Second Year - 1st Semester', so the entire first-year foundation (intro CS, Calc I/II, intro physics) is absent from the shown progression; and the plan is conspicuously physics-heavy (PHYS 234/335/430/432/442/485, OPSE 310) for a program titled CS + Applied Mathematics, with relatively few upper-level applied-math courses actually appearing in the plan of study, so the mapping between the two named degrees is not obvious.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the missing first-year term and clearly label which courses satisfy the CS degree versus the Applied Mathematics degree so the dual-degree progression is legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The deepest prerequisite chain is 7, deep but reasonable for a 5-year combined degree, and the sample prerequisite strings are well-formed with explicit boolean logic and grade thresholds. However, missingCourseCount is 8 with dangling references CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, and PHYS 231A. Most are placeholder/category codes (GER 200/300, MATH 300/400 elective tiers) or lab co-sections (CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) rather than genuinely broken course prerequisites, which softens the impact but still leaves the graph incompletely resolved.
dimensions.suggestion: Map the 8 dangling codes to real catalog entries or explicitly mark GER/elective tiers as category placeholders so the prerequisite graph resolves cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives - only a plan of study and course list. For a catalog page, the absence of stated outcomes is a real finding: prospective students and reviewers cannot see what competencies the combined CS + Applied Mathematics degree is designed to produce.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program-level learning outcomes/educational objectives for the combined degree, ideally distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The stated total of 134 credits is appropriate for a 5-year combined dual degree. However, creditReconcileDelta is -33 (summed term credits 101 versus stated 134), a large discrepancy driven primarily by the parsed plan omitting the first-year term rather than by a curriculum flaw. As presented, the plan does not reconcile to its stated total, which undermines transparency of how credits are distributed across general education, CS core, math, and physics.
dimensions.suggestion: Include the missing first-year term so summed term credits reconcile to the stated 134, and show a summary breakdown by category (GER, CS core, math/applied-math, physics, electives).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The plan is organized into labeled terms with per-term credit totals, which helps. But the page has notable parsing/formatting defects: catalog URLs are split across multiple rows as fragmentary null-code items (e.g., 'History and Humanities GER 200 level (http://catalog...' followed by 'general-education-requirements/ger-200-level/)'), and 'or ...' alternatives are rendered as standalone null-code line items rather than being attached to their base course. There is also no program-level overview/description and no first-year term, leaving the page incomplete and harder to read.
dimensions.suggestion: Repair the parsed rows so alternative courses ('or MATH 328', 'or COM 313') and split URLs render as part of their parent requirement, and add an introductory program description.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Required and elective coursework reflects current practice well: CS 301 Introduction to Data Science, CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity, CS 356 Computer Networks, CS 435 Advanced Data Structures and Algorithms, CS 331 Databases, and CS 490/491 software-engineering and senior-project work, complemented by strong computational math/physics (MATH 340 Applied Numerical Methods, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling of Applied Physics Problems). The main modern gap is the absence of a dedicated machine-learning/AI course and any cloud/DevOps exposure, but the data-science plus cybersecurity plus computational-modeling combination is contemporary and marketable.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or cross-list a machine-learning/AI elective to close the most visible modern gap and reinforce the data-science emphasis.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sample course descriptions are consistently present, specific, and informative rather than thin or placeholder - e.g., CS 241, CS 301, CS 341, CS 435, and the MATH sequence all carry substantive topic-level detail. A minor blemish is bleed-through text in a few excerpts (MATH 222's excerpt runs into unrelated 'MATH 225. Survey of Probability...' content), but overall description quality is strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Stated prerequisites are generally clear and internally consistent, using explicit grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and sensible AND/OR logic (e.g., CS 341: '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280'). Clarity is reduced by the 8 dangling references at the program level (labs and GER/elective placeholders such as PHYS 231A, GER 200/300, MATH 300/400) and by 'or' alternatives that appear as detached null-code items, which weaken end-to-end consistency.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes/educational objectives for the combined CS + Applied Mathematics degree, distinguishing the computing and applied-math competencies.
Reconcile the credit plan: add the missing first-year term so summed credits meet the stated 134 (currently 101, creditReconcileDelta -33) and present a category-level credit breakdown.

Resolve the 8 dangling course references (CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) by mapping labs/electives to real codes or flagging them as category placeholders.
Fix presentation defects: reattach 'or ...' alternatives and split catalog URLs to their parent requirements and add an introductory program description.
Clarify the dual-degree structure by labeling which courses satisfy CS versus Applied Mathematics, since the visible plan reads as physics-heavy relative to the program title.

b-s-in-data-science-268.name: B.S. in Data Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is logically ordered and builds cleanly from foundations to advanced data-science work: CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288 -> CS 435 for computing, a parallel math spine (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 244 -> 341 plus 337 Linear Algebra and 478 Stat Methods), converging on the DS core (DS 340, CS 370 AI, CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 450 Data Visualization, CS 444 Big Data Systems) and a CS 492 capstone. Term loads are steady (14-16 credits) with no abrupt difficulty jumps. The main weakness is the truncated Fourth Year 2nd Semester, which shows only an HSS senior seminar and a free elective and omits the second capstone term, leaving the culminating sequence visually incomplete.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the complete Fourth Year 2nd Semester (including the second capstone course and remaining electives) so the senior year and capstone sequence read as a finished progression.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible and well-formed. deepestPrereqChain is 7 (e.g., CS 444 and CS 450), which is appropriate depth for terminal capstone-level courses in a four-year BS and does not indicate over-constraining; foundational and mid-level courses sit at shallow, reasonable depths. missingCourseCount is 6 (GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), but every dangling reference is peripheral rather than a core node: GER 200/GER 300 are general-education level placeholders, PHYS 111A/121A are co-requisite lab sections, and YWCC 207/307 are one-credit professional-development seminars. The core CS/DS/MATH prerequisite chains themselves resolve cleanly with consistent grade gates.
dimensions.suggestion: Eliminate the six dangling references (GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) by linking the lab/seminar codes to their catalog entries and re-tagging GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories rather than course codes.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives, and no program description or overview of any kind. For a catalog page this absence is a genuine gap: a prospective student can infer the intended skills only by reading the individual course list, with no stated statement of what a graduate will know or be able to do. The strong, current course roster partially implies the intended competencies, but nothing is made explicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program-level learning outcomes (e.g., 4-8 statements covering statistical foundations, machine learning, data engineering/big data, visualization, and ethics) alongside a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Across the terms that parsed, the distribution is balanced and transparent (14-16 credits each, with a healthy mix of general education, math, computing core, and data-science electives). However, credit accounting cannot be reconciled: statedTotalCredits is null (the program total did not parse cleanly) and creditReconcileDelta is therefore null. summedTermCredits is only 105.0, well below a typical ~120-125 credit BS, and that undercount is largely because the Fourth Year 2nd Semester is incomplete. The internal balance is adequate, but the missing total and unreconcilable sum are a real credit-transparency defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Parse and display a single stated total program credit count and verify that the summed term credits reconcile to it (currently statedTotalCredits is null and the summed 105 excludes an incomplete final term).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: While the terms are clearly labeled and most course rows carry code, title, and credits, the page has multiple structural presentation defects. General-education requirement rows are broken across two items with catalog URLs bleeding into the title text (e.g., 'History and Humanities GER 200 level (http://catalog.njit.edu/...' followed by a second row 'general-education-requirements/ger-200-level/)'), several such rows carry null credits, some titles are truncated or abbreviated ('Computing & Effective Com', 'Stat Methods in Data Sci'), the FYS seminar row has a null code, and the final term is incomplete. Combined with the absent program description, these issues meaningfully hurt readability and completeness.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study rendering: merge each split GER row into a single readable requirement line, strip raw URLs out of titles, restore full course titles, and fill in the null credit values.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required and elective courses reflect current data-science practice strongly. The core spans machine learning (CS 375), artificial intelligence (CS 370), data mining (CS 482), big data systems (CS 444), data visualization (CS 450), database design (CS 331), a dedicated data-science foundations course (DS 340), and a two-part capstone (CS 492), supported by a solid statistics/linear-algebra spine (MATH 244, 337, 341, 478) and systems programming (CS 288 Linux). This is a modern, employable stack. The gaps are marginal: there is no course explicitly named for deep learning, cloud/MLOps, or data engineering pipelines, though ML/AI and Big Data partially cover this ground.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or explicitly surface an elective in deep learning and one in cloud/data-engineering (pipelines, MLOps) to close the most visible modern gaps relative to current industry hiring.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 38 sampled courses carry present, specific, and informative descriptions with no placeholder or missing-description entries. Most descriptions run to the excerpt cap (~220 characters) and convey real topical content (e.g., CS 375 ML, DS 340 data-science fundamentals, CS 444 big data). A few math entries are terse (MATH 111 at 89 characters: 'limits, differentiation, applications of differentiation, and integration'), but even these are accurate and usable rather than thin or empty.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with explicit grade gates (predominantly 'with a grade of C or better') and sensible boolean logic across alternatives (e.g., CS 280: 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent'). Chains are internally consistent. Minor blemishes keep it from a 5: phrasing varies ('grade C or better' vs 'grade of C or better' vs 'grade of C or higher'), CS 444 contains a duplicated-word typo ('all with with a grade of C or better'), and six referenced codes (GER 200/300, PHYS 111A/121A, YWCC 207/307) are dangling, though these are peripheral GER/lab/seminar placeholders rather than core prerequisites.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes and a short program overview; the data currently contains no stated outcomes, objectives, or description.

Fix credit transparency: parse and display a single stated total credit count and reconcile it against summed term credits (statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is only 105 and excludes an incomplete final term).

Repair the plan-of-study presentation: merge split GER requirement rows, strip catalog URLs out of title text, restore truncated course titles, fill null credits, and complete the Fourth Year 2nd Semester.

Resolve the six dangling course references (GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) by linking labs/seminars to catalog entries and re-tagging GER levels as requirement categories, not course codes.
Add or surface electives in deep learning and cloud/data-engineering (pipelines, MLOps) to close the most visible gaps against current data-science industry practice.

b-s-in-human-computer-interaction-247.name: B.S. in Human-Computer Interaction

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan builds a clear HCI spine: foundational computing and web work (CS 100, IS 117, IS 218) leads into the UX thread (IS 247 Designing the User Experience -> IS 375 Discovering User Needs for UX -> IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX), reinforced by psychology/cognitive-science and human-factors courses (PSY 210, Cognitive Processes, IE 355) and a senior project (IS 491). Progression is well ordered and the deepestPrereqChain of 7 confirms genuine vertical depth. Coherence is weakened only by unspecified 'HCI Specialization Elective 1/2' slots and several parsed-in cross-listed Rutgers options that make a few term-to-term transitions harder to read.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit course lists for the 'HCI Specialization Elective' slots and annotate the intended UX-research sequence (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448) so the core progression is unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is largely sensible: prerequisites use explicit grade conditions and boolean logic (e.g., IS 218 requires '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and intro courses correctly carry none. Of 27 referenced courses only 3 are missing from the catalog (missingCourseCount=3): GER 300 is a general-education placeholder rather than a real course, and YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are 1-credit professional-development seminars absent from the scraped undergraduate catalog. No core course is dangling. The main caution is the deepestPrereqChain of 7 (IS 448 sits at depth 7), which is on the deep side for an undergraduate elective and risks over-constraining scheduling.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the three dangling references — add YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 to the catalog and replace the 'GER 300' placeholder with a linked GER requirement — and audit the depth-7 chain into IS 448 to confirm it is not over-constrained.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present anywhere in the provided data, and there is no program description either. For a catalog page this is a real gap: prospective students and assessors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do, which is especially notable for a professional UX/HCI degree where competency statements (user research, interaction design, usability evaluation) would be expected.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit, measurable set of program learning outcomes (e.g., conduct user research, design and prototype interfaces, plan and analyze usability studies) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits (120) equals statedTotalCredits (120), so creditReconcileDelta is 0.0 — internally consistent. Term loads are evenly distributed across the four years (14-16 credits per semester), and the split spans general education, a substantial HCI/computing core, psychology/design support courses, and electives. The transparency of the elective portion is the only limitation: six 'General Elective' slots plus two unspecified 'HCI Specialization Elective' slots make it hard to see exactly how major-vs-free credits are apportioned.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the balanced, reconciling term structure but add a summary credit-breakdown (GER vs. major core vs. specialization vs. free electives) and enumerate the elective pools so the distribution is fully transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled (First Year — 1st Semester ... Fourth Year — 2nd Semester) and per-term credit totals are present, which aids legibility. However, the page lacks any program overview/description, and several plan-of-study lines are garbled artifacts: GER requirements are split across multiple rows with broken URL fragments (e.g., '...general-' / 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)'), alternative courses appear as standalone rows ('or MATH 138 or General Calculus I'), and 'Select one of the following:' is rendered as an item with credits. These fragments would surface as noise on a catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a concise program overview paragraph and clean up the plan so GER links, 'or' alternatives, and 'select one of' groupings render as structured options rather than fragmented separate rows.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum maps closely to current UX/HCI practitioner roles: dedicated user research (IS 375 Discovering User Needs for UX), interaction/interface design (IS 247), usability testing and UX measurement (IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX), human factors/ergonomics (IE 355), plus supporting cognitive psychology, statistics, database, and web-application skills. This is a modern, coherent UX pipeline. Gaps relative to current practice are the absence of explicit accessibility, contemporary prototyping tooling (e.g., Figma), and any AI/ML-in-UX or data-informed design content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or flag electives covering accessibility/inclusive design, industry-standard prototyping tools, and AI/ML in UX to keep the degree aligned with current hiring expectations.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 24 sample courses carry substantive, specific descriptions rather than thin or placeholder text — e.g., IS 448 explains usability and UX research, IS 375 frames anticipating user needs, and CS 100 details problem-solving and program control flow. None are missing or obviously stub descriptions. Only minor caveats: several AD/IE courses lead with enrollment 'Restrictions' text before the substantive content, and the provided data is excerpt-truncated, so full-length completeness cannot be fully confirmed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally stated unambiguously with explicit grade conditions and boolean structure (e.g., ENGL 102 'with a grade of C or higher', IS 218 '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and intro courses correctly list none. Clarity is reduced slightly by a few category-style prerequisites that name broad requirement buckets ('Statistics GUR (MATH 105, ...)', 'Computing Literacy GER course') and by the three peripheral dangling references (GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), but no contradictions or nonsensical grade conditions appear.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit, measurable program-level learning outcomes for the HCI degree (user research, interaction design, usability evaluation) — none currently exist on the page.
Add a program overview description and clean up garbled plan-of-study entries (fragmented GER URL rows, 'or' alternatives, and 'select one of the following' lines).
Resolve the three dangling course references — add YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 to the catalog and replace the 'GER 300' placeholder with a linked GER requirement.
Enumerate the 'HCI Specialization Elective' and 'General Elective' pools with concrete course lists and add a GER/core/elective credit-breakdown summary.
Refresh industry currency by adding explicit accessibility, prototyping-tool, and AI/ML-in-UX coverage to the required or elective offerings.

b-s-in-information-technology-254.name: B.S. in Information Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan follows a clear, well-ordered IT progression: foundations (IT 101, CS 100, IT 120) lead into programming (CS 113, IT 114), then design/web (IT 201, IT 202), then systems and networks (IT 340, IT 420, IS 331), and finally integration and a capstone (IT 490 Systems Integration, IT 491 IT Capstone Project). Difficulty ramps sensibly term over term with no abrupt jumps, and professional-skills and ethics courses (YWCC 207/307, COM 313, IS 350) are threaded in appropriately. The main weakness is that eight 'Specialization Course' slots and seven 'General Elective' slots are unresolved placeholders, so the specialization pathway that gives the degree its shape is not visible on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Name the concrete specialization tracks and the courses each contains (with any track-internal prerequisites) so the 24 credits of specialization coursework read as a coherent pathway rather than generic placeholders.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible and well-formed: the deepest chain is 6 (culminating in IT 490 Systems Integration, which requires CS 113/115, IS 331/CS 331, and IT 340), a reasonable depth for a four-year program that shows genuine layering without over-constraining. Prerequisites throughout carry explicit grade conditions and clear OR-alternatives (e.g., IT 114 requires CS 113 or CS 115). missingCourseCount is 4, but the dangling references (GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are general-education placeholders and 1-credit YWCC professional-development seminars rather than core IT prerequisites, so they do not break the technical dependency chain.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references (GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) by linking the GER placeholders to their category pages and ensuring the YWCC 207/307 seminar codes match catalog entries, so the prerequisite graph has zero unresolved references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data. The plan lists courses and credits but never states what a graduate should know or be able to do (e.g., competencies in networking, system administration, database design, or web application development). For a catalog page, the absence of explicit outcomes is a real deficiency that prevents students, accreditors, and employers from understanding the program's intended competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a short set of explicit, measurable program learning outcomes (e.g., 'design and administer networked systems', 'build multi-tier web applications', 'apply computing ethics') mapped to the core IT course sequence.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credits reconcile exactly: summedTermCredits is 120 against a stated total of 120, giving a creditReconcileDelta of 0.0 (internally consistent). The per-term load is well balanced (15, 16, 15, 13, 15, 16, 15, 15), staying within a normal full-time range with no overloaded or underweight semester. The split across general-education (ENGL, MATH, science, HSS/GER), IT major core, specialization courses, and general electives is transparent and appropriately proportioned across the four years.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a one-line credit-category summary (e.g., major core vs. specialization vs. GER vs. free electives) so the already-balanced distribution is visible at a glance without summing the term tables.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The overall structure is legible: terms are clearly labeled by year and semester, credits are shown per item and per term, and core courses carry codes and titles. However, there are notable parsing/presentation artifacts: general-education requirements are broken across multiple rows with catalog URLs bleeding into the title text (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER (http://catalog.njit.edu/...' followed by a stray 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)' row with a null credit), several items have null course codes, and at least one title is truncated ('Computing & Effective Com'). These flaws hurt readability even though the plan is followable.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the GER rows so each requirement is a single line with a proper hyperlinked label (not raw URL fragments split across rows), and expand truncated titles like 'Computing & Effective Com' to their full names.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required coursework maps well to current IT practice: networking (IT 120, IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks), Linux system administration (IT 340), database design and management (IS 331), modern web/internet applications (IT 202), systems integration across platforms and e-commerce (IT 490), computing ethics and society (IS 350), and a team-based capstone (IT 491). Professional communication (COM 313 Technical Writing) and YWCC professional-development seminars add employability value. The emphasis is contemporary and job-aligned; the main uncertainty is that cloud, security, and data-analytics currency depends on the unspecified specialization tracks, which are not shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Surface the specialization-track catalog so modern in-demand areas (cloud/DevOps, cybersecurity, data analytics) are explicitly represented and clearly current rather than hidden behind generic 'Specialization Course' placeholders.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled course descriptions are present, specific, and informative rather than thin or placeholder. Each conveys concrete topics and scope, e.g., IT 340 lists Linux booting, process control, file systems, and user/resource management; IT 202 covers multi-tier web architecture with hands-on modern web languages; IS 331 explains database back-end functionality for analysis and decision-making. Excerpts are truncated (a display artifact) and a couple carry minor stray text (MATH 138 ends with '298 Jordan'), but substance and specificity are consistently strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously and are internally consistent. Grade conditions are explicit (e.g., CS 113 requires 'CS 100 or CS 103 with a grade C or better'), alternative paths are spelled out with OR logic (IT 114: 'CS 113 or CS 115'; IT 202 allows a department-approved high-level-language course), and true entry-level courses correctly show no prerequisite (CS 100, IT 101, IT 120, MATH 105). Deductions are minor: some prerequisites reference GER-category placeholders (e.g., COM 313/312 require a 'History and Humanities GER 200 level' course) that tie into the four dangling references rather than concrete course codes.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit, measurable program-level learning outcomes mapped to the core IT sequence — the biggest gap, since none are currently present.

Resolve the specialization pathway by naming the actual tracks and their courses so the 24 credits of 'Specialization Course' placeholders (and 21 credits of 'General Elective') become a visible, coherent, industry-current curriculum.
Fix the four dangling course references (GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) so the prerequisite/requirement graph has zero unresolved codes.

Clean up presentation artifacts: collapse the split GER rows with embedded URL fragments into single hyperlinked requirement lines, remove null-code stray rows, and expand truncated titles like 'Computing & Effective Com'.
Add a credit-category summary (major core vs. specialization vs. GER vs. free electives) to make the already-consistent 120-credit distribution transparent at a glance.

b-s-in-science-technology-society-and-b-.name: B.S. in Science, Technology & Society and B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The visible course set shows well-layered, logically building sequences: a computing spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114/IT 114) feeds a web/application track (IS 117 -> IS 218/219 -> IS 322/331/373 -> IS 421) and an advanced-IS/analytics tier (IS 390 -> IS 455, IS 344 -> IS 465), alongside a coherent business foundation (ACCT, ECON, FIN, HRM, ENTR). The STS side is represented by ethics/society content (IS 350). A deepest prerequisite chain of 7 confirms genuine multi-year layering rather than a flat catalog. The main limitation is that only a single plan-of-study group (2nd Semester) was parsed, so full term-over-term ordering cannot be directly verified from the data.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the complete term-by-term plan of study for both degrees so progression, co-requisite timing, and difficulty ramp can be seen across all eight semesters rather than a single term fragment.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible and richly specified: deepestPrereqChain is 7, which is deep but appropriate for a combined four-year degree, and prerequisites use explicit boolean logic and grade conditions (e.g., IS 218 requires '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; CS 113 'with a grade C or better'). Only 3 of 59 referenced courses are missing from the catalog (missingCoursesSample: YWCC 107, YWCC 207, YWCC 307), roughly 5 percent, and these appear to be YWCC seminar/orientation-type courses rather than technical gateways, so the dangling references are low-impact. The chief watch-item is that several capstone-level courses (IS 333, IS 448, IS 465) sit at depth 7, which can create scheduling bottlenecks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the 3 dangling references (YWCC 107, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) by adding catalog entries or correcting the codes, and document recommended sequencing for the depth-7 courses to avoid senior-year bottlenecks.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives are present anywhere in the provided data. For a catalog-quality page, and especially for a combined degree that must articulate how STS and Business/Information Systems competencies integrate, the absence of stated outcomes is a real deficiency: prospective students and assessors cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program-level learning outcomes for the combined degree, ideally mapping each outcome to the STS, business, and information-systems course blocks that develop it.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: summedTermCredits is 12 against a statedTotalCredits of 120, giving a creditReconcileDelta of -108. Per the combined/dual-degree caveat this large negative delta is primarily a data-consistency artifact of only one plan-of-study group (2nd Semester, 12 cr) being parsed and of overlapping plans, not proof of a curriculum defect. Nonetheless, with only a single term captured, the split across general education, major core, and electives cannot be assessed for transparency or balance, and the stated total does not tie out to the visible plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore and reconcile the full plan of study so summed term credits equal the stated 120 (delta 0), and present a clear breakdown of general-education vs. major-core vs. elective credits for each of the two degrees, flagging shared/overlapping courses.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The program page is incomplete and partly garbled as captured. Only one requirement group ('2nd Semester') is present, and it contains vague or malformed items: placeholder titles like 'General Elective 5 2' and 'General Elective 6 2', null course codes, a 'Select one of the following:' line with no grouped options, and an 'or IT 491 or IT Capstone Project' alternative rendered as a stray line. IS 491 also shows a null credit value within the group. While the underlying individual course descriptions are strong, the plan-of-study presentation itself is fragmentary, unlabeled beyond one term, and does not read as a finished catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the plan-of-study display with all terms labeled, real elective titles and credit values, and cleanly grouped 'select one of' alternatives (e.g., IS 491 / IT 491) so the page is legible and complete.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course inventory reflects current information-systems and business practice well: web and mobile application development (IS 218/219, IS 322), database design (IS 331), cloud-based web systems for startups (IS 421), UX design and usability measurement (IS 247, IS 375, IS 448), social network analysis and web mining/information retrieval (IS 333, IS 392), content management systems (IS 373), e-commerce (IT 310), and data-analysis/data-science foundations (IS 465). The business complement (accounting, finance, fintech restrictions, entrepreneurship) plus STS ethics (IS 350) gives graduates a modern, employable blend. The offering could still be strengthened with more explicit contemporary data science, machine learning, and security content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or highlight current-practice electives in machine learning/data analytics and information security to keep the technical side aligned with present IS/BIS hiring demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled course descriptions are overwhelmingly present, specific, and informative, conveying concrete topics and skills (e.g., IS 331 on relational databases and back-end functionality, IS 421 on cloud-based web systems in a startup context, IS 448 on usability and UX measurement). Restrictions and audience notes are included where relevant. A few entries are thin, notably IS 489 ('Students continue their research in preparation for completing a Research Thesis.'), but these are the exception rather than the norm.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally stated unambiguously with explicit boolean logic and grade thresholds (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)', 'CS 113 with a grade C or better'). GUR/GER-based prerequisites (IS 333, IS 448, IS 465) enumerate the full list of acceptable courses, which is verbose but internally consistent. The only clarity/consistency drag is the 3 dangling YWCC references (YWCC 107/207/307) elsewhere in the referenced set, though none appears in the sampled prerequisite strings.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes for the combined degree, mapping each to the STS, business, and information-systems course blocks that develop it (currently none are stated).

Restore and reconcile the full plan of study so summed term credits equal the stated 120 credits (creditReconcileDelta currently -108, driven by only one parsed term); this fixes both the credit-balance and presentation gaps.

Clean up the plan-of-study presentation: replace placeholder items ('General Elective 5 2', 'General Elective 6 2'), fill null course codes and the null credit on IS 491, and cleanly group the 'select one of' alternatives (IS 491 / IT 491).
Resolve the 3 dangling course references (YWCC 107, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) by adding catalog entries or correcting the codes.
Add or spotlight current-practice electives in machine learning/data analytics and information security, and document sequencing for the depth-7 capstone courses to prevent senior-year bottlenecks.

b-s-in-web-information-systems-258.name: B.S. in Web & Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan of study is well sequenced term over term with a clear web/IS spine: CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114/IT 114 for programming, and IS 117 (Intro Website Development) -> IS 218 (Building Web Applications) -> IS 219 (Adv Website Development) -> IS 421 (Advanced Web Applications) for the web track, plus a coherent UX progression (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX). Foundations (calculus, statistics, ENGL 101/102) land early and advanced electives (IS 322, IS 333, IS 373, IS 392) in later years. The main weakness is that only 7 of the expected 8 semesters are present — the Fourth Year 2nd (final) semester is absent from the data — leaving the capstone term of the progression unverifiable.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing Fourth Year 2nd (final) semester so the full eight-term progression, including any senior capstone, is visible and can be checked end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is mostly sensible and uses explicit boolean structure (e.g., IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)') with grade thresholds, and foundational courses correctly show no prerequisites. However metrics.deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 333 sits at depth 7), which is on the deep side and partly inflated by long GUR-style prerequisite lists, and metrics.missingCourseCount is 4 with missingCoursesSample [GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307]: GER 200/GER 300 are General-Education-Requirement placeholders mis-read as course codes, while YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are real required 1-credit professional-development courses that dangle because they are absent from the undergraduate catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries for YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 (or link the correct codes) and treat GER 200/GER 300 as GER placeholders rather than course codes, then re-check whether the depth-7 chain reflects genuine sequencing or over-constraint from bundled GUR lists.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives — only the plan-of-study term structure and course descriptions. For a catalog-quality page, the absence of stated outcomes is a real gap: a prospective student cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit, numbered list of program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives (e.g., design and build web-based information systems, apply UX research and usability evaluation, manage databases) so the page states measurable expected competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit transparency is compromised: statedTotalCredits is null (the plan-of-study total did not parse cleanly) and metrics.creditReconcileDelta is null, so the plan cannot be reconciled to a stated total. The seven visible terms sum to only 108 credits (metrics.summedTermCredits = 108) — well short of a typical ~120-124 credit B.S. — consistent with the missing final semester. Per-term loads among the visible terms are balanced (15-16 credits each) and the major/GER/elective mix looks reasonable, but the missing total and missing term make overall balance impossible to confirm.
dimensions.suggestion: Parse and display the official stated total credits and include the final term so summedTermCredits reconciles to that total (delta = 0), giving a transparent, verifiable credit distribution.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The core course rows are clean, but the plan-of-study parsing introduces noticeable garbling: General-Education-Requirement rows are split across multiple items with catalog URLs bleeding into the course list (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER (http://catalog.njit.edu/...' followed by a stray 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)' row), alternative-course lines appear as orphan items ('or CS 114 or Introduction to Computer Science II'), and several titles are abbreviated ('Adv Website Development', 'Computing & Effective Com'). Combined with the null total credits and the missing final semester, the page reads as incompletely cleaned.
dimensions.suggestion: Post-process the plan of study to collapse split GER/URL fragments into single labeled placeholder rows, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent course, and expand truncated titles for a clean, readable table.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly current and industry-aligned. It covers modern web engineering (Building Web Applications, Advanced Web Applications, Content Management Systems), mobile (IS 322 Mobile Applications), data-facing skills (IS 331 Database Design, IS 392 Web Mining and Information Retrieval, IS 333 Social Network Analysis), a full UX arc (Designing the User Experience, Discovering User Needs for UX, Usability & Measuring UX), plus E-Commerce Technology and professional/ethics content (IS 350, COM 312/313, ENTR/IE management). This maps directly to real web, UX, and information-systems roles.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the strong UX and web-engineering core and consider surfacing current stack/tooling (e.g., cloud deployment, APIs, front-end frameworks) explicitly in course descriptions to signal currency to prospective students.

descriptionQuality.justification: Across the 38 sampled courses, descriptions are consistently present and substantive — the shown excerpts run the full ~220-character sample window with concrete, informative content (e.g., IS 218 'a critical, hands-on introduction to the design of Web-based Information Systems', IS 392 covering 'design, implementation and evaluation of search engines and web mining applications'). No missing or stub descriptions were observed; a minor deduction is that some entries lead with restriction/prerequisite prose before describing the course.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite text is generally clear and machine-parseable, with explicit boolean structure and grade thresholds (e.g., IS 218/IS 219 '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)', CS 113 'CS 100 or CS 103 with a grade C or better'), and true foundation courses correctly carry no prerequisite. Clarity dips only where GUR requirements are inlined as long alternation lists (IS 333, IS 350), which are accurate but verbose and harder to read at a glance.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives — none are present in the data.
Fix credit transparency: parse and show the official stated total credits and add the missing Fourth Year 2nd (final) semester so the 108 summed credits reconcile to the real total (delta = 0).
Clean up plan-of-study presentation: collapse split GER rows and bleeding catalog URLs, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent course, and expand truncated course titles.

Resolve the 4 dangling references — add catalog entries for the required YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 professional-development courses, and treat GER 200 / GER 300 as GER placeholders rather than course codes.
Review the depth-7 prerequisite chain (e.g., IS 333) to confirm it reflects genuine sequencing rather than over-constraint from bundled GUR prerequisite lists.
